We rarely encounter a more serious introduction: there is no truth of greater importance. Let everyone hear this and apply it to themselves. The poor are in danger from excessive desire for the wealth of the world, just as rich people are from excessive delight in it. The psalmist begins by applying it to himself, and that is the right way to treat of Divine things. Before he describes the folly of carnal security, he lays down, from his own experience, the benefit and comfort of a holy, gracious security, which those enjoy who trust in God, not in their worldly wealth. In the day of judgment, the sin of our past actions will surround us. In those days, worldly, wicked people will be afraid; but why should a person fear death who has God with him?

Here is a description of the spirit and way of worldly people. A person may have wealth, and may have their heart enlarged in love, thankfulness, and obedience, and may do good with it. Therefore, it is not that people have riches that proves them to be worldly, but that they set their hearts upon them as the best things. Worldly men have only some fleeting thoughts of the things of God, while their fixed thoughts, their inward thoughts, are about the world; that lies nearest the heart. But even with all their wealth they cannot save the life of the dearest friend they have. This looks further, to the eternal redemption to be wrought out by the Messiah. The redemption of the soul shall cost very dear; but, once wrought, it shall never need to be repeated. And he, the Redeemer, shall rise again before he sees corruption, and then shall live forevermore. This also shows the folly of worldly people, who sell their souls for that which will never buy them. With all their wealth they cannot secure themselves from the stroke of death. Yet one generation after another applaud their principles; and the character of a fool, as drawn by heavenly Wisdom itself, continues to be followed even among professed Christians. Death will ask the proud sinner, Where is your wealth, your pomp? And in the morning of the resurrection, when all who sleep in the dust shall awake, the upright shall be advanced to the highest honor, when the wicked shall be filled with everlasting shame and contempt. Let us now judge of things as they will appear on that day. The beauty of holiness is that alone which the grave cannot touch or damage.

Believers should not fear death. The distinction of people's outward conditions, however great in life, makes no difference at death; but the difference of people’s spiritual states, though in this life it may seem of small account, yet at and after death is very great. The soul is often put for the life. The God of life, who was its Creator at first, can and will be its Redeemer at last. It includes the salvation of the soul from eternal ruin. Believers will be under strong temptation to envy the prosperity of sinners. People will praise you and commend you, saying that you have done well for yourself in raising an estate and family. But what will it avail to be approved of people if God condemns us? Those who are rich in the graces and comforts of the Spirit have something that death cannot strip from them, nay, which death will improve; but as for worldly possessions, just as we brought nothing into the world, so it is certain that we shall carry nothing out; we must leave all to others. The sum of the whole matter is, that it can profit a person nothing to gain the whole world, to become possessed of all its wealth and all its power, if they lose their own soul, and are cast away for want of that holy and heavenly wisdom which distinguishes people from the brutes, in their life and at their death. And are there people who can prefer the lot of the rich sinner to that of poor Lazarus, in life and death, and to eternity? Surely there are. What need then we have of the teaching of the Holy Ghost; when, with all our boasted powers, we are prone to such folly in the most important of all concerns!
